questions.question: What does the FIRST set of a non-terminal contain?
options: All non-terminals that can appear first in a derivation
All terminals that can begin any string derived from that non-terminal
All tokens that appear at the start of the input
The first production rule listed for that non-terminal
questions.explanation: FIRST(X) is the set of terminals that can appear as the first symbol of a string derived from X. If X can derive ε, then ε is also in FIRST(X). FIRST sets are used to build the predictive parsing table.

questions.question: What does the FOLLOW set of a non-terminal A contain?
options: All terminals that can appear immediately after A in some sentential form
All non-terminals that A can derive
All terminals that A can derive as the leftmost symbol
The set of productions involving A
questions.explanation: FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of A in some sentential form. The end marker $ is always in FOLLOW of the start symbol. FOLLOW sets are used when a production derives ε to decide which lookahead tokens trigger that production.

questions.question: A grammar is LL(1) if and only if:
options: Every non-terminal has exactly one production
The predictive parsing table has at most one production per cell — no FIRST/FIRST or FIRST/FOLLOW conflicts
The grammar contains no ε-productions
Every terminal appears in exactly one production
questions.explanation: LL(1) requires that for every non-terminal A and lookahead token a, there is at most one production A → α with a in FIRST(α) (or in FOLLOW(A) if α ⇒* ε). Conflicts mean the parser cannot decide deterministically.

questions.question: During LL(1) parsing with a stack, when the stack top is a non-terminal X and the current token is 'a', what does the parser do?
options: Pop X and push the production from table[X][a] in reverse order
Pop X and read the next token
Pop X and push all productions for X onto the stack
Backtrack and try a different production
questions.explanation: When the stack top is a non-terminal, the parser looks up table[X][current_token]. If a production X → Y₁ Y₂ … Yₖ is found, it pops X and pushes Yₖ, …, Y₂, Y₁ (reverse order) so that Y₁ is on top. This ensures leftmost derivation order.

questions.question: What does LL(1) mean in the context of parsing?
options: Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1 token lookahead
Left-to-right scan, Left-associative, 1 production rule
Linear time, Logarithmic space, 1 pass
Left-to-right scan, Longest match, 1 symbol
questions.explanation: LL(1) means: reading input Left-to-right, producing a Leftmost derivation, with 1 token of lookahead. The parser uses a predictive table to choose which production to apply based on the current nonterminal and the next token. No backtracking is needed.
